sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has an orange background with: - Top green bar (row 0) - likely timer/progress - Charcoal 3x4 + Magenta 4x4 stacked at cols 36-39, rows 1-7 - likely the player avatar - Blue 4x20 horizontal bar at rows 16-19 - a platform/target - Two yellow L-shaped blocks at bottom rows 52-59 - obstacles/platforms - Bottom gray bar (rows 60-63) - floor
- Goal model: Unclear yet. Need to test movement to understand mechanics.
- Plan: Move RIGHT x2 and UP x2 to reach the target area.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 39 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after DOWN - the blue bar position and also check if the blue bar changed shape or something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after DOWN
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation['nodes']

print("BEFORE DOWN:")
for node in before_seg:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  {node['color']}: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}], pixels={node['pixels']}")

print("\nAFTER DOWN:")
for node in after_seg:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  {node['color']}: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}], pixels={node['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so DOWN DID work - the blue bar moved from rows 12-15 to rows 16-19. So vertical movement does work. But UP didn't work when the blue bar was at rows 12-15. That means there's something blocking upward movement at that position.
Wait, let me think about this differently. When I first tested UP, the blue bar was at rows 16-19 and moved to rows 12-15. Then I did RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP. After that, the blue bar was at rows 12-15, cols 20-39. So the RIGHT moves worked, but the UP moves didn't. Now when I tried UP again, it didn't move. But DOWN did work, moving it back to rows 16-19.
So the blue bar can move DOWN but not UP from rows 12-15. What's blocking it? Looking at the board:
- The blue bar at rows 12-15, cols 20-39
- The charcoal is at rows 1-3, cols 36-39
- The magenta is at rows 4-7, cols 36-39
There's nothing between rows 8-11 in the cols 20-39 range. So why can't the blue bar move up?
Wait, maybe the blue bar can only move horizontally? Let me reconsider the first UP test. When I tested UP initially, the blue bar was at rows 16-19, cols 12-31, and it moved to rows 12-15, cols 12-31. But now at rows 12-15, cols 20-39, it can't move up. And it CAN move down.
Hmm, maybe there's a gravity mechanic? The blue bar falls down but can't go up past a certain point? Or maybe the blue bar can only move horizontally, and the initial UP movement was actually something else?
Wait, let me re-examine. In the first UP test, I had the blue bar at rows 16-19 and it moved to rows 12-15. That's clearly upward movement. So UP does work. But now it doesn't work from rows 12-15.
Could there be an invisible barrier? Or maybe the blue bar can only move up to a certain row? Let me try moving the blue bar to a different column position and then trying UP.
Actually, let me reconsider the game. Maybe the blue bar is a "slider" that can only move horizontally, and the initial UP test was actually a different kind of movement. Let me check if the blue bar can move UP from its current position (rows 16-19, cols 20-39).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try UP from current position (rows 16-19, cols 20-39)
action(['UP'])

# Check result
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in seg:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if node['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"Blue bar after UP: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content: Blue bar after UP: row=[12,15], col=[20,39]
